title: Messaging & Integration (SQS, SNS, EventBridge, Step Functions)

sections.title: 🧒 The Story — Why Decoupling Matters
sections.rawMarkdown:
```
TIGHTLY COUPLED (bad):
Order Service → directly calls → Payment Service
                              → directly calls → Email Service
                              → directly calls → Inventory Service

PROBLEM:
→ Payment Service is slow → Order Service waits → users wait
→ Email Service crashes → Order Service gets error → order fails?
→ Add new service → change Order Service code
→ Spike: 1000 orders → all 3 services overwhelmed simultaneously

LOOSELY COUPLED (good):
Order Service → puts message → SQS Queue
                              → SNS Topic
                              → EventBridge

Each service reads at its OWN PACE.
Email crashes → message waits in queue → email processes later → no order lost.
Add new service → just subscribe to the queue → Order Service unchanged.
Spike: 1000 orders → queue buffers → each service processes at capacity.
```

---

LOOSELY COUPLED (good):

Email crashes → message waits in queue → email processes later → no order lost.
Add new service → just subscribe to the queue → Order Service unchanged.
Spike: 1000 orders → queue buffers → each service processes at capacity.

sections.title: 📬 SQS — Simple Queue Service
sections.rawMarkdown:
### Core Concepts
```
SQS = a message queue
Producer sends messages → Queue holds them → Consumer reads + deletes them

ANATOMY OF AN SQS MESSAGE:
→ Body: your data (JSON, text, XML — up to 256 KB)
→ Message ID: unique identifier
→ Receipt Handle: token needed to delete the message
→ Attributes: metadata (timestamp, type, deduplication ID)
→ Message Group ID: for FIFO ordering (FIFO queues only)

LARGE MESSAGES (> 256 KB):
→ Store payload in S3
→ Put S3 reference in SQS message
→ Consumer reads message → fetches actual data from S3
→ Library: SQS Extended Client (handles this automatically)
```

### Visibility Timeout — Critical Concept

```
WHAT IT IS:
When consumer reads a message → message becomes INVISIBLE to other consumers
→ Consumer has [visibility timeout] seconds to process + delete
→ If not deleted in time → message becomes visible again → another consumer picks it up

GOAL: prevent two consumers from processing the same message simultaneously

DEFAULT: 30 seconds

TOO SHORT:
→ Processing takes 40 seconds
→ Visibility timeout = 30 seconds
→ Message reappears after 30 seconds
→ ANOTHER consumer picks it up
→ Same message processed TWICE → duplicate processing bug

TOO LONG:
→ Consumer crashes after 5 seconds of processing
→ Visibility timeout = 10 minutes
→ Message hidden for 10 minutes before others can try
→ Slow recovery from consumer failures

SET IT TO: 1.5x your expected processing time
If processing takes 2 minutes → set visibility timeout to 3 minutes
Consumer can also extend visibility timeout while processing if needed

ReceiveMessage call: up to 10 messages at once (batch reading)
DeleteMessage: must call after processing (with receipt handle)
ChangeMessageVisibility: extend timeout while processing long jobs
```

### Standard vs FIFO Queue

```
STANDARD QUEUE:
→ Nearly unlimited throughput (thousands/second)
→ At-least-once delivery (may receive same message more than once!)
→ Best-effort ordering (not guaranteed — messages may arrive out of order)
→ USE FOR: high-throughput, order doesn't matter, can handle duplicates

HANDLING DUPLICATES (standard queue):
→ Make your processing IDEMPOTENT
→ "Process payment for order123" received twice → only charge once
→ Check: "did I already process order123?" before processing
→ Use DynamoDB: if order123 exists in processed table → skip

FIFO QUEUE:
→ Exactly-once processing (no duplicates guaranteed by SQS)
→ First-in, first-out ordering guaranteed
→ Lower throughput: 300 messages/second (or 3,000 with batching)
→ Name must end with .fifo: MyQueue.fifo
→ MORE EXPENSIVE than standard
→ USE FOR: financial transactions, ordered workflows, when duplicates cause problems

FIFO DEDUPLICATION:
→ Deduplication ID: if you send same ID within 5 minutes → SQS deduplicates
→ Content-based deduplication: SQS hashes message body → auto deduplication
```

### Dead Letter Queue (DLQ)

```
WHAT IT IS:
Messages that fail processing repeatedly → moved to DLQ (a separate queue)

HOW IT WORKS:
1. Consumer reads message → processing fails → doesn't delete message
2. Message becomes visible again → another attempt
3. After maxReceiveCount attempts (e.g., 3) → moved to DLQ automatically

WHY IT MATTERS:
Without DLQ:
→ Bad message → processing fails → retries infinitely → blocks queue → disaster

With DLQ:
→ Bad message → 3 failures → DLQ
→ Main queue continues processing other messages
→ You can inspect DLQ: "why did these messages fail?"
→ Fix the bug → redrive from DLQ to main queue → reprocess

BEST PRACTICES:
→ ALWAYS configure DLQ for production queues (non-negotiable)
→ Set CloudWatch alarm on DLQ message count > 0
   (any messages in DLQ = something is failing → alert!)
→ DLQ should have LONGER retention than main queue
   Main: 4 days default. DLQ: 14 days (max)
→ DLQ type must MATCH main queue type (Standard DLQ for Standard, FIFO DLQ for FIFO)
```

### Long Polling vs Short Polling

```
SHORT POLLING (bad default):
→ SQS.receiveMessage() → immediately returns (even if queue empty)
→ Empty response → your code loops → calls again → empty → loops...
→ Burning API calls, burning money, burning CPU

LONG POLLING (always use this):
→ SQS.receiveMessage({ WaitTimeSeconds: 20 })
→ If queue empty → SQS waits up to 20 seconds for message
→ Message arrives → returns immediately
→ No message in 20 seconds → returns empty response
→ Reduces API calls by 10-20x
→ Reduces cost significantly
→ Reduces CPU usage in consumer

SET LONG POLLING: either per-request (WaitTimeSeconds) or per-queue
Recommended: WaitTimeSeconds = 20 (maximum)
```

### SQS Pricing
```
Standard: $0.40 per million requests (first 1M free/month)
FIFO:     $0.50 per million requests (no free tier)
Data: FREE within same region
→ SQS is extremely cheap — rarely a cost concern
```

---

sections.title: 📢 SNS — Simple Notification Service
sections.rawMarkdown:
### What is SNS?
```
SNS = pub/sub messaging (publish/subscribe)

SQS: one-to-one (producer → queue → one consumer)
SNS: one-to-many (publisher → topic → ALL subscribers simultaneously)

ANALOGY:
SQS = numbered ticket queue at a deli counter (one person serves one ticket)
SNS = PA system announcement (everyone hears it at the same time)
```

### SNS Components

```
TOPIC:
→ A named channel for messages
→ Publishers send TO the topic
→ Subscribers receive FROM the topic

PUBLISHER:
→ Any AWS service or your code
→ CloudWatch Alarm → SNS (alert everyone)
→ S3 Event → SNS (new file uploaded)
→ Your code → SNS (order placed)

SUBSCRIBER (multiple per topic):
→ SQS Queue (most reliable — message persists)
→ Lambda Function (process immediately)
→ HTTP/HTTPS endpoint (webhook to external service)
→ Email (direct email to person)
→ SMS (text message — very expensive per message!)
→ Mobile Push (iOS/Android push notification)
→ Kinesis Data Firehose (streaming to S3/Redshift)

DELIVERY:
→ SNS attempts delivery to ALL subscribers simultaneously
→ If subscriber is unavailable: retries (SQS never loses, HTTP/Lambda might)
→ For reliability: use SNS → SQS (fan-out pattern) not SNS → Lambda directly
```

### SNS + SQS Fan-Out Pattern

```
BEST PRACTICE PATTERN:

Publisher → SNS Topic
            → SQS Queue A (Email Service consumer)
            → SQS Queue B (Inventory Service consumer)
            → SQS Queue C (Analytics Service consumer)

WHY SQS in the middle (not Lambda directly)?
→ SQS persists messages → if Email Service is down → message waits
→ Email Service restarts → reads from queue → no message lost
→ Each service processes at its own pace independently
→ Easy to add new subscriber (new SQS queue) without changing publisher
→ DLQ per queue → independent failure handling per service

ORDER PLACED EXAMPLE:
Order placed → SNS
  → SQS (Payment service): process payment
  → SQS (Inventory service): reduce stock count
  → SQS (Email service): send confirmation email
  → SQS (Analytics service): record conversion event

Each runs independently. Payment failure doesn't affect email sending.
```

### SNS Message Filtering

```
Without filtering: EVERY subscriber receives EVERY message.
With filtering: subscriber only receives messages matching its filter.

Filter Policy (JSON on the subscription):
{
  "status": ["SHIPPED", "DELIVERED"],
  "region": ["us-east-1"]
}
→ This subscriber only receives messages where status is SHIPPED or DELIVERED
   AND region is us-east-1

USE CASE:
Shipping events → SNS
→ SQS (Tracking service): filter: status=["SHIPPED", "IN_TRANSIT", "DELIVERED"]
→ SQS (Customer email): filter: status=["SHIPPED", "DELIVERED"] only (not IN_TRANSIT)
→ SQS (Analytics): no filter (receives all events)

→ Shipping service doesn't send separate messages per subscriber
→ Each subscriber gets exactly what it needs
→ Reduces noise, reduces processing cost
```

---

blocks.content: ### What is SNS?

SNS = pub/sub messaging (publish/subscribe)

SQS: one-to-one (producer → queue → one consumer)
SNS: one-to-many (publisher → topic → ALL subscribers simultaneously)

ANALOGY:
SQS = numbered ticket queue at a deli counter (one person serves one ticket)
SNS = PA system announcement (everyone hears it at the same time)
### SNS Components

TOPIC:
→ A named channel for messages
→ Publishers send TO the topic
→ Subscribers receive FROM the topic

PUBLISHER:
→ Any AWS service or your code
→ CloudWatch Alarm → SNS (alert everyone)
→ S3 Event → SNS (new file uploaded)

SUBSCRIBER (multiple per topic):
→ SQS Queue (most reliable — message persists)
→ Lambda Function (process immediately)
→ HTTP/HTTPS endpoint (webhook to external service)
→ Email (direct email to person)
→ SMS (text message — very expensive per message!)
→ Mobile Push (iOS/Android push notification)
→ Kinesis Data Firehose (streaming to S3/Redshift)

DELIVERY:
→ SNS attempts delivery to ALL subscribers simultaneously
→ If subscriber is unavailable: retries (SQS never loses, HTTP/Lambda might)
→ For reliability: use SNS → SQS (fan-out pattern) not SNS → Lambda directly
### SNS + SQS Fan-Out Pattern

BEST PRACTICE PATTERN:

Publisher → SNS Topic
            → SQS Queue A (Email Service consumer)
            → SQS Queue B (Inventory Service consumer)
            → SQS Queue C (Analytics Service consumer)

WHY SQS in the middle (not Lambda directly)?
→ SQS persists messages → if Email Service is down → message waits
→ Email Service restarts → reads from queue → no message lost
→ Each service processes at its own pace independently
→ Easy to add new subscriber (new SQS queue) without changing publisher
→ DLQ per queue → independent failure handling per service

ORDER PLACED EXAMPLE:
Order placed → SNS
  → SQS (Payment service): process payment
  → SQS (Inventory service): reduce stock count
  → SQS (Email service): send confirmation email
  → SQS (Analytics service): record conversion event

Each runs independently. Payment failure doesn't affect email sending.
### SNS Message Filtering

Without filtering: EVERY subscriber receives EVERY message.
With filtering: subscriber only receives messages matching its filter.

Filter Policy (JSON on the subscription):
{
  "status": ["SHIPPED", "DELIVERED"],
  "region": ["us-east-1"]
}
→ This subscriber only receives messages where status is SHIPPED or DELIVERED
   AND region is us-east-1

USE CASE:
Shipping events → SNS
→ SQS (Tracking service): filter: status=["SHIPPED", "IN_TRANSIT", "DELIVERED"]
→ SQS (Customer email): filter: status=["SHIPPED", "DELIVERED"] only (not IN_TRANSIT)
→ SQS (Analytics): no filter (receives all events)

→ Publishers send TO the topic
→ Subscribers receive FROM the topic
→ Any AWS service or your code
→ CloudWatch Alarm → SNS (alert everyone)
→ S3 Event → SNS (new file uploaded)
→ Your code → SNS (order placed)
→ SQS Queue (most reliable — message persists)

sections.title: 🚌 EventBridge — Event Bus
sections.rawMarkdown:
### What is EventBridge?
```
EventBridge = serverless event bus that connects AWS services, SaaS apps, and your apps

MORE POWERFUL THAN SNS:
→ Rule-based routing with complex pattern matching
→ 100+ event sources (all AWS services, Salesforce, Zendesk, GitHub, etc.)
→ Schema registry (discover and document event shapes)
→ Event replay (archive and replay past events)
→ Cross-account, cross-region event routing

SNS: publisher explicitly sends to topic
EventBridge: events happen → bus routes them based on rules

ANALOGY:
SNS = walkie-talkie (you send to specific channel, all listeners hear it)
EventBridge = smart router (events flow in, rules decide who gets what)
```

### EventBridge Components

```
EVENT BUS:
→ Default bus: receives events from AWS services (EC2 state change, S3 events, etc.)
→ Custom bus: your application events
→ Partner bus: SaaS events (Salesforce, Zendesk, GitHub, etc.)

EVENT:
→ JSON object describing something that happened
→ Source: "aws.ec2" or "myapp.orders"
→ detail-type: "EC2 Instance State-change Notification"
→ detail: { instance-id: "i-abc", state: "terminated" }

RULE:
→ Pattern to match events → target to route them to
→ Pattern examples:
  { "source": ["aws.ec2"], "detail": { "state": ["terminated"] } }
  → Match: any EC2 termination event

TARGETS (10+ per rule):
→ Lambda (most common — process the event)
→ SQS (queue it for async processing)
→ SNS (fan-out from here)
→ Step Functions (start a workflow)
→ ECS task (run a container task)
→ API Gateway (call HTTP endpoint)
→ Another event bus (cross-account routing)
→ Kinesis (stream to data pipeline)
```

### EventBridge Big Picture Diagram

```
                    AWS SERVICES / YOUR APPS / SaaS
                 ┌──────────────┬──────────────┬──────────────┐
                 │ S3 PutObject │ EC2 stopped  │ OrderPlaced  │
                 └──────┬───────┴──────┬───────┴──────┬───────┘
                        │              │              │
                        ▼              ▼              ▼
                   ┌────────────────────────────────────────┐
                   │          EVENTBRIDGE BUS              │
                   │ default bus / custom bus / partner bus│
                   └────────────────────────────────────────┘
                              │            │            │
                              ▼            ▼            ▼
                        Rule A match   Rule B match  Rule C match
                       source=S3       state=stopped event=OrderPlaced
                              │            │            │
                              ▼            ▼            ▼
                           Lambda         SNS      Step Functions
                                            │
                                            ▼
                                      SQS fan-out

MENTAL MODEL:
Events are not sent to one consumer directly.
Events land on the bus first.
Rules inspect the event and decide who should receive it.
```

### EventBridge vs SNS vs SQS

```
USE SQS WHEN:
→ One producer, one consumer type (or small fan-out via SNS)
→ Need to buffer/throttle workload
→ Consumer must process at its own pace
→ Need retries, DLQ for failures
→ Simple async decoupling

USE SNS WHEN:
→ Fan-out: one event → multiple consumers simultaneously
→ Push notifications (mobile, SMS, email)
→ Simple pub/sub without complex routing

USE EVENTBRIDGE WHEN:
→ Complex event routing (content-based routing with patterns)
→ Events from AWS services, SaaS, or multiple producers
→ Cross-account or cross-region event routing
→ Need schema registry (document event shapes)
→ Need event replay (replay past events for new subscribers)
→ Scheduled events (cron-like: "every Monday 9am → run this")
→ Building event-driven microservices architecture

EVENTBRIDGE SCHEDULER (separate from event bus):
→ Cron-like scheduling: invoke Lambda, ECS task, API at specific times
→ One-time or recurring
→ More reliable than Lambda + CloudWatch Events (old way)
→ Use for: nightly batch jobs, weekly reports, scheduled API calls
```

---

MORE POWERFUL THAN SNS:

→ Pattern examples:

→ Lambda (most common — process the event)

                        ▼              ▼              ▼
                   ┌────────────────────────────────────────┐
                   │          EVENTBRIDGE BUS              │
                   │ default bus / custom bus / partner bus│
                   └────────────────────────────────────────┘

                              ▼            ▼            ▼

                                            ▼
                                      SQS fan-out

Events land on the bus first.
Rules inspect the event and decide who should receive it.

USE SQS WHEN:
→ One producer, one consumer type (or small fan-out via SNS)
→ Need to buffer/throttle workload
→ Consumer must process at its own pace
→ Need retries, DLQ for failures
→ Simple async decoupling

USE SNS WHEN:
→ Fan-out: one event → multiple consumers simultaneously
→ Push notifications (mobile, SMS, email)
→ Simple pub/sub without complex routing

USE EVENTBRIDGE WHEN:
→ Complex event routing (content-based routing with patterns)
→ Events from AWS services, SaaS, or multiple producers
→ Cross-account or cross-region event routing
→ Need schema registry (document event shapes)
→ Need event replay (replay past events for new subscribers)
→ Scheduled events (cron-like: "every Monday 9am → run this")
→ Building event-driven microservices architecture

EVENTBRIDGE SCHEDULER (separate from event bus):
→ Cron-like scheduling: invoke Lambda, ECS task, API at specific times
→ One-time or recurring
→ More reliable than Lambda + CloudWatch Events (old way)

sections.title: 🔄 Step Functions — Workflow Orchestration
sections.rawMarkdown:
### What is Step Functions?
```
Step Functions = visual workflow orchestration for distributed applications

PROBLEM IT SOLVES:
Complex multi-step processes:
1. Validate order
2. Check inventory
3. Process payment
4. Reserve inventory
5. Send confirmation email
6. Schedule delivery

WITHOUT Step Functions:
→ Lambda A calls Lambda B calls Lambda C...
→ Error handling: manual try/catch in each Lambda
→ State tracking: where is this order in the process?
→ Retry logic: if step 4 fails, retry 3 times then...
→ Visibility: impossible to see what's happening
→ Modifications: change one step → change all downstream code

WITH Step Functions:
→ Visual state machine (you can literally see the workflow)
→ Each step is a state
→ Built-in error handling (catch, retry with exponential backoff)
→ State persisted automatically
→ CloudWatch integration (see execution history)
→ Change workflow in one place
```

### Step Functions Big Picture Diagram

```
OrderPlaced Event
      │
      ▼
┌───────────────────┐
│ Start Execution   │
└─────────┬─────────┘
          ▼
   ┌───────────────┐
   │ ValidateOrder │
   └──────┬────────┘
          ▼
   ┌───────────────┐
   │ Choice State  │
   │ valid or not? │
   └───┬───────┬───┘
       │yes    │no
       ▼       ▼
┌────────────┐  ┌──────────────┐
│ ProcessPay │  │ Reject Order │
└─────┬──────┘  └──────┬───────┘
      ▼                ▼
┌───────────────┐   ┌───────┐
│ Parallel State│   │ Fail  │
│ reserve + mail│   └───────┘
└──────┬────────┘
       ▼
┌───────────────┐
│ Schedule Ship │
└──────┬────────┘
       ▼
   ┌───────┐
   │Succeed│
   └───────┘

MENTAL MODEL:
Step Functions is not a queue.
It is the traffic controller for a business process.
It remembers state between steps, retries safely, and shows where the workflow failed.
```

### State Machine Concepts

```
STATE MACHINE = the workflow definition (JSON or visual)

STATE TYPES:
Task State:
→ Do something: invoke Lambda, call API, run ECS task, call DynamoDB
→ Wait for result → proceed to next state

Choice State:
→ If/else branching based on data
→ If order.amount > 1000 → high-value path
→ Else → standard path

Wait State:
→ Pause for time OR until timestamp
→ "Wait 1 hour then send follow-up email"

Parallel State:
→ Execute multiple branches simultaneously
→ Wait for ALL to complete → continue
→ "While processing payment AND reserving inventory → both complete → next"

Map State:
→ Iterate over array, process each item
→ "For each item in cart → check inventory"

Succeed/Fail State:
→ Terminal states — workflow complete or failed

Pass State:
→ Pass input to output, optionally transform
→ Useful for testing, data transformation

EXAMPLE FLOW:
OrderPlaced → ValidateOrder
ValidateOrder → Choice:
  Order valid → ProcessPayment
  Order invalid → SendRejection → Fail

ProcessPayment → Choice:
  Payment success → Parallel[ReserveInventory, SendConfirmation]
  Payment failed → RetryPayment (retry 3 times, 30s backoff) → Fail

Parallel finishes → ScheduleDelivery → Succeed
```

### Express vs Standard Workflows

```
STANDARD WORKFLOWS:
→ Duration: up to 1 year
→ At-least-once execution (may duplicate on failure)
→ Execution history: 90 days (visible in console)
→ Pricing: $0.025 per 1,000 state transitions
→ USE FOR: long-running, critical workflows (order processing, approval flows)

EXPRESS WORKFLOWS:
→ Duration: up to 5 minutes
→ At-least-once execution
→ No execution history UI (use CloudWatch)
→ Pricing: $1.00 per million executions + duration
→ USE FOR: high-volume, short-duration workflows (IoT data processing, streaming)

SYNCHRONOUS EXPRESS:
→ Caller waits for result (request/response pattern)
→ API Gateway → Step Functions → wait → return result
→ Useful for: multi-step APIs that look synchronous to the caller

ASYNCHRONOUS EXPRESS:
→ Fire and forget
→ Caller gets execution ID, polls for result
→ Useful for: long background tasks
```

---

blocks.content: ### What is Step Functions?

Step Functions = visual workflow orchestration for distributed applications

PROBLEM IT SOLVES:
Complex multi-step processes:
1. Validate order
2. Check inventory
3. Process payment
4. Reserve inventory
5. Send confirmation email
6. Schedule delivery

WITHOUT Step Functions:
→ Lambda A calls Lambda B calls Lambda C...
→ Error handling: manual try/catch in each Lambda
→ State tracking: where is this order in the process?
→ Retry logic: if step 4 fails, retry 3 times then...
→ Visibility: impossible to see what's happening
→ Modifications: change one step → change all downstream code

WITH Step Functions:
→ Visual state machine (you can literally see the workflow)
→ Each step is a state
→ Built-in error handling (catch, retry with exponential backoff)
→ State persisted automatically
→ CloudWatch integration (see execution history)
→ Change workflow in one place
### Step Functions Big Picture Diagram

OrderPlaced Event
      │
      ▼
┌───────────────────┐
│ Start Execution   │
└─────────┬─────────┘
          ▼
   ┌───────────────┐
   │ ValidateOrder │
   └──────┬────────┘
          ▼
   ┌───────────────┐
   │ Choice State  │
   │ valid or not? │
   └───┬───────┬───┘
       │yes    │no
       ▼       ▼
┌────────────┐  ┌──────────────┐
│ ProcessPay │  │ Reject Order │
└─────┬──────┘  └──────┬───────┘
      ▼                ▼
┌───────────────┐   ┌───────┐
│ Parallel State│   │ Fail  │
│ reserve + mail│   └───────┘
└──────┬────────┘
       ▼
┌───────────────┐
│ Schedule Ship │
└──────┬────────┘
       ▼
   ┌───────┐
   │Succeed│
   └───────┘

MENTAL MODEL:
Step Functions is not a queue.
It is the traffic controller for a business process.
It remembers state between steps, retries safely, and shows where the workflow failed.
### State Machine Concepts

STATE MACHINE = the workflow definition (JSON or visual)

STATE TYPES:
Task State:
→ Do something: invoke Lambda, call API, run ECS task, call DynamoDB
→ Wait for result → proceed to next state

Choice State:
→ If/else branching based on data
→ If order.amount > 1000 → high-value path
→ Else → standard path

Wait State:
→ Pause for time OR until timestamp
→ "Wait 1 hour then send follow-up email"

Parallel State:
→ Execute multiple branches simultaneously
→ Wait for ALL to complete → continue
→ "While processing payment AND reserving inventory → both complete → next"

Map State:
→ Iterate over array, process each item
→ "For each item in cart → check inventory"

Succeed/Fail State:
→ Terminal states — workflow complete or failed

Pass State:
→ Pass input to output, optionally transform
→ Useful for testing, data transformation

EXAMPLE FLOW:
OrderPlaced → ValidateOrder
ValidateOrder → Choice:
  Order valid → ProcessPayment
  Order invalid → SendRejection → Fail

ProcessPayment → Choice:
  Payment success → Parallel[ReserveInventory, SendConfirmation]
  Payment failed → RetryPayment (retry 3 times, 30s backoff) → Fail

Parallel finishes → ScheduleDelivery → Succeed
### Express vs Standard Workflows

STANDARD WORKFLOWS:
→ Duration: up to 1 year
→ At-least-once execution (may duplicate on failure)
→ Execution history: 90 days (visible in console)
→ Pricing: $0.025 per 1,000 state transitions
→ USE FOR: long-running, critical workflows (order processing, approval flows)

EXPRESS WORKFLOWS:
→ Duration: up to 5 minutes
→ At-least-once execution
→ No execution history UI (use CloudWatch)
→ Pricing: $1.00 per million executions + duration
→ USE FOR: high-volume, short-duration workflows (IoT data processing, streaming)

SYNCHRONOUS EXPRESS:
→ Caller waits for result (request/response pattern)
→ API Gateway → Step Functions → wait → return result
→ Useful for: multi-step APIs that look synchronous to the caller

ASYNCHRONOUS EXPRESS:
→ Fire and forget
→ Caller gets execution ID, polls for result
→ Useful for: long background tasks

sections.title: 🎯 Interview Q&A — Messaging
sections.rawMarkdown:
**Q: SQS vs SNS vs EventBridge — explain the difference and when to use each.**
```
"Three different messaging patterns for different needs.

SQS is a point-to-point queue. One producer sends messages, one type of
consumer processes them. The queue buffers messages so consumers can work
at their own pace. Use it when you need to decouple and buffer work between
two services, and when retries and DLQ matter (payment processing, file processing).

SNS is pub/sub fan-out. One message goes to ALL subscribers simultaneously.
Use it when one event needs to trigger multiple services at the same time —
order placed needs to trigger payment, inventory, email, analytics all at once.
Fan-out pattern: SNS + multiple SQS queues is the most reliable version.

EventBridge is an intelligent event bus with content-based routing.
Events flow in from 100+ sources, rules match patterns, and route to targets.
Use when you need complex routing logic, events from AWS services (EC2 state
changes, S3 events), SaaS integrations, or event replay capabilities.

In practice: SQS for buffering between two services, SNS for fan-out,
EventBridge for complex event-driven architectures and AWS service events.
Often used together: EventBridge routes events to SNS which fans out to SQS."
```

**Q: How would you handle a situation where SQS messages are failing repeatedly?**
```
"That's exactly what Dead Letter Queues are for — and they're mandatory in production.

Configure DLQ on the main queue with maxReceiveCount = 3.
After 3 failed processing attempts, SQS moves the message to the DLQ.
Set a CloudWatch alarm: ApproximateNumberOfMessagesVisible on DLQ > 0.
The moment a message lands in DLQ → alarm fires → team is notified.

Investigation: read the DLQ message → look at the payload → find the problem.
Common causes: malformed JSON, unexpected data format, dependency service down,
code exception that wasn't caught.

Fix the consumer code. Then use SQS redrive: move messages from DLQ back to
main queue. Consumer processes them successfully this time.

Without DLQ: bad messages would retry infinitely, block the queue, and a single
malformed message could bring down your entire processing pipeline."
```

**Q: Queue backlog is growing fast, but consumer CPU is normal. What do you investigate?**
```
"Normal CPU does not mean the consumer is healthy enough for backlog.

I would check:
→ queue depth and age of oldest message
→ consumer concurrency / number of workers
→ downstream dependency latency
→ visibility timeout too short causing reprocessing
→ batch size too small
→ poison messages causing repeated retries

This often happens when consumers are waiting on database or third-party API calls.
CPU stays low because workers are blocked, not busy.

The senior response is to find the throughput limiter end to end, not just add more consumers blindly."
```

**Q: How do you design messaging so one bad downstream service does not take the whole platform down?**
```
"Use isolation boundaries in the event flow.

Patterns:
→ SNS fan-out to separate SQS queue per consumer
→ each queue gets its own retry policy and DLQ
→ slow email service should not block billing or inventory
→ Step Functions for explicit compensating actions where workflow matters

If every consumer shares one queue or one synchronous chain,
one bad dependency becomes everyone's outage.

The mature design principle is failure isolation first, feature fan-out second."
```

**Q: Ordering, retries, and exactly-once delivery keep coming up in interviews. How do you answer clearly?**
```
"I start by correcting the premise: in distributed systems, exactly-once is usually a business-level design goal, not a transport guarantee.

SQS Standard:
→ at-least-once
→ best-effort ordering

SQS FIFO:
→ ordered within message group
→ exactly-once processing still requires idempotent consumer logic

So my answer is:
→ choose FIFO only when ordering is a real requirement
→ keep message groups practical so throughput does not collapse
→ build idempotent consumers because retries and duplicates still matter at application level

Senior interviewers usually want to hear that you understand delivery semantics plus application safeguards, not just product marketing."
```

---

"Three different messaging patterns for different needs.

SQS is a point-to-point queue. One producer sends messages, one type of
consumer processes them. The queue buffers messages so consumers can work
at their own pace. Use it when you need to decouple and buffer work between

SNS is pub/sub fan-out. One message goes to ALL subscribers simultaneously.
Use it when one event needs to trigger multiple services at the same time —

Fan-out pattern: SNS + multiple SQS queues is the most reliable version.

EventBridge is an intelligent event bus with content-based routing.
Events flow in from 100+ sources, rules match patterns, and route to targets.
Use when you need complex routing logic, events from AWS services (EC2 state
changes, S3 events), SaaS integrations, or event replay capabilities.

In practice: SQS for buffering between two services, SNS for fan-out,
EventBridge for complex event-driven architectures and AWS service events.
Often used together: EventBridge routes events to SNS which fans out to SQS."
**Q: How would you handle a situation where SQS messages are failing repeatedly?**

"That's exactly what Dead Letter Queues are for — and they're mandatory in production.

Configure DLQ on the main queue with maxReceiveCount = 3.

**Q: Queue backlog is growing fast, but consumer CPU is normal. What do you investigate?**

"Normal CPU does not mean the consumer is healthy enough for backlog.

I would check:
→ queue depth and age of oldest message

→ downstream dependency latency
→ visibility timeout too short causing reprocessing
→ batch size too small
→ poison messages causing repeated retries

This often happens when consumers are waiting on database or third-party API calls.
CPU stays low because workers are blocked, not busy.

The senior response is to find the throughput limiter end to end, not just add more consumers blindly."
**Q: How do you design messaging so one bad downstream service does not take the whole platform down?**

**Q: Ordering, retries, and exactly-once delivery keep coming up in interviews. How do you answer clearly?**

→ at-least-once
→ best-effort ordering

→ ordered within message group
→ exactly-once processing still requires idempotent consumer logic

So my answer is:
→ choose FIFO only when ordering is a real requirement
→ keep message groups practical so throughput does not collapse
→ build idempotent consumers because retries and duplicates still matter at application level

Senior interviewers usually want to hear that you understand delivery semantics plus application safeguards, not just product marketing."

sections.title: 🔗 Connects To
sections.rawMarkdown:
```
SQS ← EC2/Lambda/ECS: consumers read from SQS
SQS ← SNS: SNS delivers to SQS (fan-out pattern)
SQS ← EventBridge: EventBridge routes events to SQS
SQS → Lambda: Lambda trigger (SQS event source mapping)
SQS → CloudWatch: queue depth alarms, DLQ alarms

SNS ← Your code/AWS services: publishers
SNS → SQS: fan-out to multiple queues
SNS → Lambda: direct invocation
SNS → Email/SMS/Push: human notifications
SNS → HTTP: webhooks

EventBridge ← All AWS services: event sources (default bus)
EventBridge ← Your app: custom events (custom bus)
EventBridge → Lambda/SQS/SNS/Step Functions/ECS: targets
EventBridge → Scheduler: cron-like job scheduling

Step Functions → Lambda: invoke function as state
Step Functions → ECS: run container task as state
Step Functions → DynamoDB/SQS/SNS: direct SDK integrations
Step Functions ← API Gateway: synchronous workflow trigger

NEXT: Lambda — Serverless Compute (Deep Dive)
Everything about Lambda — cold starts, concurrency, VPC, error handling, and more.
```

---
---
